summary: Stellar High Time & Lost Sailor/Saint sequence; tight second set
blurb: The show shines with a soaring High Time, crisp Lost Sailor > Saint transition and a soulful Gloria finale. Jerry’s vocals and guitar lead the way while Phil delivers sublime bass lines and Brent adds sparkling keyboards. The second‑set ballads and jams keep the energy high throughout.
review:
Richmond Coliseum on November 1, 1985 delivered one of the Dead’s most rewarding late‑’80 outings. From the opening “Dancin' In The Street” through a first set that built momentum with classics like “Jack Straw,” the band settled into a second set that feels like a masterclass in songcraft and improvisation. Highlights include Jerry’s beautiful solo on “High Time,” a seamless, jam‑filled transition from “Lost Sailor” into “Saint Of Circumstance,” and a soaring rendition of “Gloria” that closes the night with pure joy. Phil’s bass is described as "sublime" and "sick," especially during the bomb‑laden “Jack Straw,” while Brent’s keyboards provide heavy presence and harmonic depth throughout. Mickey and Bill lock in tight, driving rhythms—Mickey even gets forced back to his kit by the set changes, but the pre‑drums sections are praised as “amazing.” All told, the performance is a high‑energy, tightly woven set that showcases the band at peak chemistry during this era.

For listeners seeking the definitive audio experience, the crystal‑clear Oade audience tape (gd85-11-01.aud-oade.remaster.20416.sbeok.shnf) captures every nuance—from the floor‑level ambience to the vivid drum cymbals—making it the go‑to source for this show. Whether you’re a longtime Deadhead or a newcomer, this Richmond concert offers an unforgettable snapshot of the band’s late‑’80 magic.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Consistent positive sentiment across 11 of 14 recordings, with repeated praise for High Time, Lost Sailor/Saint transition, Gloria, and overall tight band playing. No recurring negative comments about performance; only recording‑quality issues noted. This strong consensus warrants a high positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear audience tape with superb ambience, captures the full energy of the show and is widely praised as one of the best 1985 audience recordings.

key_highlights: Jerry’s soaring vocals and beautiful solo on High Time
Seamless Lost Sailor > Saint Of Circumstance transition with deep jam
Gloria finale delivering a vocal howl and high‑energy climax
Tight rhythm section: Phil’s sublime bass, Brent’s sparkling keyboards, and vivid drum work

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers crystal‑clear vocals throughout, with a particularly moving solo on High Time and an unforgettable howl that lifts the entire set.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass is described as "sublime" and "sick," laying down strong bomb lines in Jack Straw and driving the groove across both sets, though one reviewer notes occasional restlessness.
band_performance.Bob: Weir shines on Spoonful with lyrical pull‑backs and gives a memorable "spelling lesson" on Gloria, adding solid rhythm work throughout.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland’s keyboards are heavy and sparkling, providing harmonies and driving the second‑set improvisations, earning praise for his overall presence.
band_performance.Mickey: Mickey delivers vivid cymbal work captured up close; despite being pushed back to the kit by set changes, his pre‑drums sections are highlighted as amazing and driving.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann locks in tight with Mickey, offering a vivid, energetic drum foundation that propels the band's jams.
